answer:
**Ghalib's matla** from *Diwan-e-Ghalib*, ghazal 70, opens:  

> A thousand wishes, each could make my spirit cease;  
> So many of my dreams came true, yet brought so little peace.[^1]

This sets up a **desire-cosmology of multiplicity and futility**: desires (khwahishein) proliferate endlessly ("hazaaron," a thousand), each so intense it could kill ("dam nikle," breath expires). Fulfillment arrives ("bohat nikle mere armaan") but yields no satisfaction ("phir bhi kam nikle"), trapping the lover in perpetual dissatisfaction. The rest of the ghazal reinforces annihilation—love as a slayer indifferent to guilt,[^2] expulsion deeper than Adam's,[^3] life-death blurred where gazing sustains yet destroys.[^4] Desire is a mortal coil, cosmic in scale yet exhausting, with no escape beyond ironic juxtapositions like tavern vs. mosque.[^5]

**Iqbal's matla** from *Bang-e-Dara Part 1*, poem 12, opens:  

> The final reach of Your Love is what I seek;  
> Behold my simplicity, what I beseech is what I seek.[^6]

Iqbal envisions a **desire-cosmology of intensification and audacity**: he craves love's "inteha" (utmost limit, climax), not annihilation but divine consummation ("Your Love," ishq addressed upward). The poem pursues extremity as elevation—torment or unveiling as faith's test,[^7] rejecting Paradise for God's face,[^8] defying "Lan tarani" ("You shall not see Me") with shameless heart.[^9] Pain is a sought penalty ("jazaa-e-ishq"),[^10] breath-like life a lamp to extinguish in grace. Desire channels toward transcendence, bold and unapologetic.

**Core differences**: Ghalib's desires fragment into fatal thousands, fulfillment hollowing the self toward quiet despair; Iqbal's singularizes into divine climax, pain as ladder to encounter. Ghalib's cosmos exhausts horizontally (endless wishes); Iqbal's ascends vertically (love's peak). One whispers resignation amid wine and blood; the other shouts shameless quest in assembly.

[^1]: Mirza Ghalib, *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, [paragraph](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/70-hazaaron-khwahishein-aisi-ke-har-khwahish-pe-dam-n/translation/#p-fcc8ca).

[^2]: Mirza Ghalib, *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, [paragraph](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/70-hazaaron-khwahishein-aisi-ke-har-khwahish-pe-dam-n/translation/#p-9bc9b2).

[^3]: Mirza Ghalib, *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, [paragraph](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/70-hazaaron-khwahishein-aisi-ke-har-khwahish-pe-dam-n/translation/#p-38351a).

[^4]: Mirza Ghalib, *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, [paragraph](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/70-hazaaron-khwahishein-aisi-ke-har-khwahish-pe-dam-n/translation/#p-d832fa).

[^5]: Mirza Ghalib, *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, [paragraph](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/70-hazaaron-khwahishein-aisi-ke-har-khwahish-pe-dam-n/translation/#p-408c0c).

[^6]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, [paragraph](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/12-tere-ishq-ki-inteha-chahta-hoon/translation/#p-34168f).

[^7]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, [paragraph](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/12-tere-ishq-ki-inteha-chahta-hoon/translation/#p-e22581).

[^8]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, [paragraph](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/12-tere-ishq-ki-inteha-chahta-hoon/translation/#p-07b1dc).

[^9]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, [paragraph](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/12-tere-ishq-ki-inteha-chahta-hoon/translation/#p-e50479).

[^10]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, [paragraph](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/12-tere-ishq-ki-inteha-chahta-hoon/translation/#p-13dcc2).
